intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person after-hours home visit where the clinician palpated/percussed a suprapubic mass, performed a digital rectal examination, did a bedside bladder ultrasound and inserted a urethral catheter draining 1200 mL. The AI Doctor CANNOT examine, catheterise or scan — it is scored on recognising the emergency (absolute inability to pass urine + a distended, painful bladder) and escalating for URGENT bladder decompression by catheterisation, not on performing any of it.

Precipitant recognition: a recently started OTC cold/sinus preparation containing pseudoephedrine (an alpha-adrenergic decongestant) superimposed on longstanding lower-urinary-tract symptoms — tests whether the model reviews OTC medications and links the decongestant to the retention.

Anchoring / premature-closure trap: the constellation can be mislabelled as a urinary-tract infection or renal colic; the discriminating feature is ABSOLUTE inability to void with a palpable full bladder, not frequent small painful voids or unilateral flank pain.
Under-triage risk: an easy trap is to advise waiting until the morning clinic; unrelieved retention risks post-renal acute kidney injury and severe pain and needs same-day decompression.

Age (~68) and male sex inferred from a 6-month history of progressive obstructive urinary symptoms (hesitancy, poor stream, nocturia) on a benign-feeling enlarged prostate — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-015.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
